2026-04-17T15:00:29.8370480Z ##[group]Run # Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8371069Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8371717Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8372110Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8372425Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8372665Z [36;1m[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8372825Z [36;1mPORT=3001[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8373007Z [36;1m[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8373174Z [36;1m# Set outputs for next steps[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8373406Z [36;1m{[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8373610Z [36;1m  echo "safe_outputs_api_key=${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8373911Z [36;1m  echo "safe_outputs_port=${PORT}"[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8374175Z [36;1m} >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"[0m
2026-04-17T15:00:29.8374379Z [36;1m[0m

2026-04-17T15:03:38.9868644Z ◆ Now let me look at the Core README more carefully and the Responses README to identify documentation gaps for the new features:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9869180Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9869721Z   1. **InboundRequestLoggingMiddleware (Core)**: The CHANGELOG mentions it was added, but the README's "Logging" section under Troubleshooting just says:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9871144Z      > "The library emits OpenTelemetry traces via the Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses and Azure.AI.AgentServer.Invocations activity sources. Enable ASP.NET Core logging in your application configuration to diagnose startup issues."
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9872097Z      
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9872410Z      This doesn't mention the new middleware at all. Users would benefit from knowing:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9872842Z      - The middleware exists and is auto-registered
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9873249Z      - What it logs (method, path, status, duration, correlation headers, trace ID)
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9873655Z      - That query strings are excluded for privacy
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9874249Z      - Log levels used (Info for 2xx, Warning for 4xx/5xx)
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9874541Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9875263Z   2. **Deterministic session ID derivation (Responses)**: The Responses README has no mention of `agent_session_id`, session ID derivation, or how sessions work at all. This is a new feature added in this push.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9876047Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9876642Z   3. **Handler-level diagnostic logging (Responses)**: The Responses README's Logging section is incomplete and doesn't cover the new diagnostic capabilities that were added.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9877311Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9877880Z   Now I need to examine the actual middleware implementation and session handling code to understand what's really being logged and how sessions are being tracked.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9878710Z

2026-04-17T15:03:38.9880390Z ✓ github-get_file_contents(owner: Azure, path: sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Res..., ref: main, repo: azure-sdk-for-net)
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9880923Z 
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9881170Z ◆ Now I have a clear picture of the documentation gaps. Let me analyze:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9881568Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9881775Z   **Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core README gaps:**
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9882061Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9882229Z   The CHANGELOG mentions:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9883258Z   1. `InboundRequestLoggingMiddleware` - logs all incoming HTTP requests with method, path, status code, duration, correlation headers, and OpenTelemetry trace ID. Successful requests log at `Information` level; 4xx/5xx responses log at `Warning` level.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9884326Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9884512Z   But the README only mentions:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9885482Z   > "The library emits OpenTelemetry traces via the `Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses` and `Azure.AI.AgentServer.Invocations` activity sources. Enable ASP.NET Core logging in your application configuration to diagnose startup issues."
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9886456Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9886607Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9887973Z   This is outdated—it doesn't cover the new middleware that automatically logs all inbound requests, the differentiated log levels based on response status, or the correlation headers being captured. The documentation needs to be updated to reflect these logging capabilities.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9889312Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9889550Z   **Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses README gaps:**
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9889857Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9890913Z   The CHANGELOG for beta.2 mentions deterministic `agent_session_id` derivation using a SHA-256 hash of `"{agent_name}:{agent_version}:{partition_hint}"` truncated to 63 lowercase hex characters, but this implementation detail isn't documented in the README either.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9891961Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9895023Z   The bigger issue is that the Core README completely omits the `InboundRequestLoggingMiddleware`—a critical feature for developers—while the Responses README is missing both the structured logging to all endpoints and the `FoundryStorageLoggingPolicy`. I need to verify whether the Responses README actually documents `agent_session_id` at all. I'm weighing which documentation gap is most critical to address. The Core package's new middleware is a foundational feature that affects all requests, but the Responses package introduced more user-facing changes with the deterministic session ID derivation and expanded logging. I need to pick the single most impactful gap to document.
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9897589Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9898343Z   The middleware in Core is the clear winner—it's a completely new auto-... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2196 chars]
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9898956Z 
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9899198Z ◆ Now I have a clear picture. The commit (#58274) added:
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9899976Z   1. A new `InboundRequestLoggingMiddleware` to Core (auto-registered, logs all HTTP requests) — **completely absent from Core README**
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9901115Z   2. Deterministic `agent_session_id` derivation in Responses — **not documented in Responses README**
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9901969Z   3. Handler-level structured logging across all Responses endpoints — **Logging section in Responses README is sparse**
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9902523Z   
2026-04-17T15:03:38.9902948Z   The most impactful gap is the Core README's missing section on the new logging middleware. Let me file the issue.

Request start logs at Information level; successful completion\\nat Information; 4xx/5xx responses at Warning. Query strings are\\ndeliberately excluded to avoid logging sensitive data.\\n\\nCo-authored-by: Copilot \\u003c223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com\\u003e\\n\\n* Add handler-level diagnostic logging to Responses endpoints\\n\\nAdd Info-level structured logs to all Responses API endpoints:\\n- POST /responses: responseId, streaming, background, store, model,\\n  conversationId, previousResponseId (moved after ID generation)\\n- POST /responses (non-streaming): final status and output count\\n- POST /responses (background): signal handoff with status\\n- GET /responses: responseId, retrieved status and output count\\n- POST /responses/{id}/cancel: responseId and result status\\n- DELETE /responses/{id}: responseId and success\\n- GET /responses/{id}/input_items: responseId\\n- Orchestrator: handler type name on invocation\\n\\nThese logs complement the Core inbound middleware (HTTP-level) with\\nresponse-specific context for debugging storage deserialization issues,\\nhandler failures, and request tracing across the full pipeline.\\n\\nCo-authored-by: Copilot \\u003c223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com\\u003e\\n\\n* Update release dates to 2026-04-17\\n\\nCo-authored-by: Copilot \\u003c223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com\\u003e\\n\\n* Address review feedback: exception handling in middleware, cache GetConversationId\\n\\n- InboundRequestLoggingMiddleware: catch exceptions from next() to force\\n  status 500 in log output instead of misleading 200; 2026-04-17T15:03:39.2213338Z {"timestamp":"2026-04-17T15:01:59.429504667Z","direction":"IN","type":"RESPONSE","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"successfully downloaded text file (SHA: [REDACTED])","type":"text"},{"resource":{"mimeType":"text/plain; charset=utf-8","text":"# Azure AI Agent Server Core library for .NET\n\nAzure.AI.AgentServer.Core is a shared hosting foundation for Azure AI Agent Server packages. It provides a library-owned ASP.NET Core host with built-in OpenTelemetry, health checks, graceful shutdown, and multi-protocol composition — so you can go from `dotnet add package` to a running agent server in minutes.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][nuget] | [Product documentation][product_doc]\n\n## Getting started\n\n### Install the package\n\nInstall the library for .NET with [NuGet](https://www.nuget.org/):\n\n```dotnetcli\ndotnet add package Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core --prerelease\n```\n\n### Prerequisites\n\n- An [Azure subscription](https://azure.microsoft.com/free/dotnet/)\n- [.NET 8](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) or later\n\n\u003e **Upgrading from a version prior to beta.21?** The package has been redesigned as a lightweight hosting\n\u003e foundation. Protocol logic has moved to [`Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses`][responses] and\n\u003e [`Azure.AI.AgentServer.Invocations`][invocations]. See the [Migration Guide][migration] for details.\n\n### Start a server (recommended)\n\nUse the builder pattern to create a server. Protocol packages provide extension methods (`AddResponses\u003cT\u003e()`, `AddInvocations\u003cT\u003e()`) to register their endpoints:\n\n```C# Snippet:Core_ReadMe_CreateBuilder\nvar builder = AgentHost.CreateBuilder();\n\n// Register protocol endpoints (protocol packages provide extension methods).\nbuilder.RegisterProtocol(\"MyProtocol\", endpoints =\u003e\n{\n    endpoints.MapGet(\"/hello\", () =\u003e \"Hello from the agent server!\");\n});\n\nvar app = builder.Build();\napp.Run();\n```\n\nThis starts a Kestrel server with OpenTelemetry, a `/readiness` health endpoint, and the `x-platform-server` identity header.\n\n## Key concepts\n\n### AgentHost\n\nThe static entry point. `AgentHost.CreateBuilder()` returns an `AgentHostBuilder` for composing protocols and configuring the server.\n\n### AgentHostBuilder\n\nConfigures the underlying ASP.NET Core host with sensible defaults: Kestrel on the `PORT` environment variable (or 8088), OpenTelemetry traces and metrics, a `/readiness` health endpoint, and `x-platform-server` user-agent header. Protocol packages use `RegisterProtocol()` to add their endpoints — each protocol registers its identity segment with the `ServerUserAgentRegistry`.\n\n### AgentHostApp\n\nThe built application. Call `Run()` to start listening. Wraps `WebApplication` with server-specific configuration applied.\n\n### FoundryEnvironment\n\nReads Azure AI Foundry platform variables (`FOUNDRY_*`, `PORT`, `SSE_KEEPALIVE_INTERVAL`) to resolve agent identity, listening port, and connection strings. Also detects `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` and `APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING` for telemetry configuration. Useful when your agent server runs as a hosted agent in AI Foundry.\n\n### Telemetry\n\nOpenTelemetry is configured automatically via `Azure.Monitor.OpenTelemetry.AspNetCore`. The `AgentHostTelemetry` class exposes the shared activity source and meter names (`ResponsesSourceName`, `InvocationsSourceName`, `ResponsesMeterName`, `InvocationsMeterName`) for the Responses and Invocations protocols. OTLP export is enabled when the `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` environment variable is set.\n\n### Health endpoint\n\nA `/readiness` endpoint is registered by default, responding to liveness and readiness probes. It reports healthy as soon as the host finishes starting.\n\n## Examples\n\nYou can familiarise yourself with different APIs using [Samples](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/tree/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core/samples).\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\n### Common errors\n\n- **Port already in use**: The server defaults to port 8088 (or the `PORT` environment variable). If the port is occupied, set `PORT` to another value or configure Kestrel directly via the builder.\n- **No protocol registered**: If you use `AgentHost.CreateBuilder()` without calling `RegisterProtocol()` (or a protocol extension method), the server will start but will have no protocol endpoints mapped.\n\n### Logging\n\nThe library emits OpenTelemetry traces via the `Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses` and `Azure.AI.AgentServer.Invocations` activity sources. Enable ASP.NET Core logging in your application configuration to diagnose startup issues.\n\n## Next steps\n\n- [Samples](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/tree/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core/samples) — Getting started, multi-protocol composition\n\n## Contributing\n\nThis project welcomes contributions and suggestions. Most contributions require you to agree to a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) declaring that you have the right to, and actually do, grant us the rights to use your contribution. For details, visit \u003chttps://cla.microsoft.com\u003e.\n\nWhen you submit a pull request, a CLA-bot will automatically determine whether you need to provide a CLA and decorate the PR appropriately (e.g., label, comment). Simply follow the instructions provided by the bot. You will only need to do this once across all repos using our CLA.\n\nThis project has adopted the [Microsoft Open Source Code of Conduct](https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/). For more information see the [Code of Conduct FAQ](https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/faq/) or contact [opencode@microsoft.com](mailto:opencode@microsoft.com) with any additional questions or comments.\n\n\u003c!-- LINKS --\u003e\n[source]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/tree/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core/src\n[nuget]: https://www.nuget.org/packages/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core\n[product_doc]: https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/foundry/agents/concepts/hosted-agents\n[migration]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core/MigrationGuide.md\n[responses]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/tree/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses\n[invocations]: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/tree/main/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Invocations\n","uri":"repo://Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/sha/[REDACTED]/contents/sdk/agentserver/Azure.AI.AgentServer.Core/README.md"},"type":"resource"}]}}}

Add the NuGet package, extend one abstract class (`ResponseHandler`), and the library handles routing, streaming (SSE), background execution, cancellation, caching, and response lifecycle management.\n\n[Source code][source] | [Package (NuGet)][nuget] | [REST API reference][rest_api] | [Product documentation][product_doc]\n\n## Getting started\n\n### Install the package\n\nInstall the library for .NET with [NuGet](https://www.nuget.org/):\n\n```dotnetcli\ndotnet add package Azure.AI.AgentServer.Responses --prerelease\n```\n\n### Prerequisites\n\n- An [Azure subscription](https://azure.microsoft.com/free/dotnet/)\n- [.NET 8.0](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download) or later\n- An ASP.NET Core application\n\n### Configure the server\n\nThe recommended way to start a Responses server is with the built-in one-line API:\n\n```C# Snippet:Responses_ReadMe_ConfigureServer_Tier1\nResponsesServer.Run\u003cEchoHandler\u003e();\n```\n\nThis starts a Kestrel server with OpenTelemetry, health checks, server user-agent headers
